question:
Let  $f:  → $  be a twice differentiable function such that  $(\sin x \cos y)(f(2x+2y) - f(2x-2y)) = (\cos x \sin y)(f(2x+2y) + f(2x-2y))$ , for all  $x, y \in $ .

If  $f'(0) = \frac{1}{2}$ , then the value of  $24 f''\left(\frac{5\pi}{3}\right)$  is:
explanation:
$(\sin x \cos y) (f(2x + 2y) - f(2x - 2y)) = (\cos x \sin y) (f(2x + 2y) + f(2x - 2y))$

$$f(2x + 2y)(\sin(x-y)) = f(2x - 2y)\sin(x + y)$$

$$\frac{f(2x+2y)}{\sin(x+y)} = \frac{f(2x-2y)}{\sin(x-y)}$$

Put  $2x + 2y = m$ ,  $2x - 2y = n$

$$\frac{f(m)}{\sin\left(\frac{m}{2}\right)} = \frac{f(n)}{\sin\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)} = K$$

$$\Rightarrow f(m) = K \sin\left(\frac{m}{2}\right)$$

$$\therefore f(x) = K \sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$$

$$f'(x) = \frac{K}{2} \cos\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$$

$$ \text{Put } x = 0; \frac{1}{2} = \frac{K}{2} \Rightarrow K = 1$$

$$f(x) = \sin\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)$$

$$f'(x) = \frac{1}{2} \cos \frac{x}{2}$$

$$f''(x) = -\frac{1}{4} \sin \frac{x}{2}$$

$$24f''\left(\frac{5\pi}{3}\right) = 24 \left(-\frac{1}{4} \sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right)\right)$$

$$= -6 \times \frac{1}{2} = -3$$
subject: Maths

question:
Let $A = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & -1 \\ 6 & \beta \end{bmatrix}$ , $\alpha > 0$ , such that $\det(A) = 0$ and

$\alpha + \beta = 1$ . If $I$ denotes $2 \times 2$ identity matrix, then the matrix $(I + A)^8$ is:
explanation:
$|A| = 0$

$$\alpha\beta + 6 = 0$$

$$\alpha\beta = -6$$

$$\alpha + \beta = 1$$

$$\Rightarrow \alpha = 3, \beta = -2$$

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$ 

$$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9-6 & -3+2 \\ 18-12 & -6+4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$ 

$$\therefore A^2 = A$$

$$A = A^2 = A^3 = A^4 = A^5 = A^n$$

$$(I + A)^8 $$

$$= {}^8C_0 I^8 A^0 + {}^8C_1 I^7 A^1 + {}^8C_2 I^6 A^2 + … + {}^8C_8 I^0 A^8$$ 

$$= {}^8C_0 I + {}^8C_1 A + {}^8C_2 A + … + {}^8C_8 A$$ 

$$= I + A ({}^8C_1 + {}^8C_2 + … + {}^8C_8)$$ 

$$= I + A (2^8 - {}^8C_0)$$ 

$$= I + A (256 - 1) = I + 255 A$$ 

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + 255 \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -2 \end{bmatrix} $$ 

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 765 & -255 \\ 1530 & -510 \end{bmatrix}$$ 

$$= \begin{bmatrix} 766 & -255 \\ 1530 & -509 \end{bmatrix}$$

title: JEE Mains 2025 Apr 2 - 2 - Maths Q12
question:
Let  $A$  be the set of all functions  $f: 
mathbb{Z} \rightarrow 
mathbb{Z}$  and  $R$  be a relation on  $A$  such that  $R = \{(f, g) : f(0) = g(1) \text{ and } f(1) = g(0)\} $. Then  $R$  is:
options: Symmetric and transitive but not reflective
Symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive
Reflexive but neither symmetric nor transitive
Transitive but neither reflexive nor symmetric
correct_answer: Symmetric but neither reflective nor transitive
explanation:
$R = \{(f, g) : f(0) = g(1) \text{ and } f(1) = g(0)\}$

Reflexive:  $(f, f) \in R$

$= f(0) = f(1)$  and  $f(1) = f(0) \rightarrow$  must hold

$\Rightarrow$  but this is not true for all function

so not reflexive

Symmetric: If  $(f, g) \in R \Rightarrow (g, f) \in R$

Now,  $g(0) = f(1)$  and  $g(1) = f(0) \rightarrow$  true

$\therefore$  symmetric

Transitive : If  $(f, g) \in R$  and  $(g, h) \in R$

$\Rightarrow (f, h) \in R$

Now  $(f, g) \in R \Rightarrow f(0) = g(1)$  and  $f(1) = g(0)$

$(g, h) \in R \Rightarrow g(0) = h(1)$  and  $g(1) = h(0)$

For  $(f, h) \in R$  we need  $f(0) = h(1)$  and  $f(1) = h(0)$

Now  $f(0) = g(1) = h(0)$  and  $f(1) = g(0) = h(1)$

Hence not transitive
subject: Maths

$$\det(A) = -4 \text{ and } A + I = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & a & 1 \ 2 & 1 & 0 \ a & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \text{ where I is the}
$$identity matrix of order $3 \times 3$ .

If $\det((a+1)\text{adj}((a-1)A))$ is $2^m 3^n$ , $m, n \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 20\}$ , then $m + n$ is equal to :

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & a & 1 \ 2 & 1 & 0 \ a & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} - I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & a & 1 \ 2 & 0 & 0 \ a & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

= 4^3 |adj 3A|

= 64  $\\times$  (3^3)^2 |A|^2

= 2^6  $\\times$  3^6  $\\times$  16

title: JEE Mains 2025 Apr 2 - 2 - Physics Q42
question:
A spherical surface separates two media of refractive indices 1 and 1.5 as shown in figure. Distance of the image of an object 'O', is :
![Diagram of a spherical surface separating two media. Medium-1 (n1=1) is on the left, and Medium-2 (n1=1.5) is on the right. An object 'O' is placed 0.2 m to the left of the surface. The center of curvature 'C' is on the right side of the surface. The radius of curvature R is 0.4 m.](https://i.ibb.co/hV72P43/spherical-surface.png)
(C is the center of curvature of the spherical surface and R is the radius of curvature)
options: 0.24 m right to the spherical surface
0.4 m left to the spherical surface
0.24 m left to the spherical surface
0.4 m right to the spherical surface
correct_answer: 0.4 m left to the spherical surface
explanation:
The formula for refraction at a spherical surface is: $\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R}$
Given:
Refractive index of medium 1, $\mu_1 = 1$
Refractive index of medium 2, $\mu_2 = 1.5$
Object distance, $u = -0.2$ m (since it is to the left of the surface and light travels from left to right)
Radius of curvature, $R = 0.4$ m (since the center of curvature is on the right side of the surface)
Substituting the values into the formula:
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} - \frac{1}{-0.2} = \frac{1.5 - 1}{0.4} $$
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} + \frac{1}{0.2} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} $$
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} - \frac{1}{0.2} $$
To subtract the fractions, find a common denominator, which is 0.4:
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{0.5}{0.4} - \frac{2}{0.4} $$
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{0.5 - 2}{0.4} $$
$$ \frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{-1.5}{0.4} $$
Now, solve for $v$:
$$ v = \frac{1.5 \times 0.4}{-1.5} $$
$$ v = -0.4 \text{ m} $$
The negative sign indicates that the image is formed on the same side as the object, which is to the left of the spherical surface. Therefore, the image is formed 0.4 m to the left of the spherical surface.

title: JEE Mains 2025 Apr 2 - 2 - Chemistry Q53
question:
The property/properties that show irregularity in first four elements of group-17 is/are :

(A) Covalent radius  
(B) Electron affinity  
(C) Ionic radius  
(D) First ionization energy

Choose the **correct** answer from the options given below:
explanation:
The order of first four elements of group-17 are as follows.

$F < Cl < Br < I$  (Covalent radius)

$Cl > F > Br > I$  (Electron affinity)

$F^- < Cl^- < Br^- < I^-$  (Ionic radius)

$F > Cl > Br > I$  (1<sup>st</sup> ionization energy)

Electron affinity order is irregular.

title: JEE Mains 2025 Apr 2 - 2 - Chemistry Q57
question: A solution is made by mixing one mole of volatile liquid A with 3 moles of volatile liquid B. The vapour pressure of pure A is 200 mm Hg and that of the solution is 500 mm Hg. The vapour pressure of pure B and the least volatile component of the solution, respectively, are :
explanation:
$P_S = P_A^0 \cdot X_A + P_B^0 \cdot X_B$

$$500 = 200 \times \frac{1}{4} + P_B^0 \cdot \frac{3}{4}$$

$$P_B^0 = 600 \text{ mm Hg}$$ 

As  $P_A^0 < P_B^0 \Rightarrow A$  is least volatile.

options: **Statement I** is correct but **Statement II** is incorrect.
Both **Statement I** and **Statement II** are incorrect
**Statement I** is incorrect but **Statement II** is correct
Both **Statement I** and **Statement II** are correct
correct_answer: Both **Statement I** and **Statement II** are correct
explanation:

If  $\Delta_o < P.E.$  , then high spin complexes are formed

If  $\Delta_o > P.E.$  , then low spin complexes are formed

question:
On complete combustion 1.0 g of an organic compound (X) gave 1.46 g of  $CO_2$  and 0.567 g of  $H_2O$ . The empirical formula mass of compound (X) is 

(Given molar mass in  $gelated{mol}^{-1}$  C : 12, H : 1, O : 16)
explanation:
$$\text{Moles of 'C'} = n_{CO_2} = \frac{1.46}{44} = 0.033$$

$$\text{Mass of 'C'} = W_c = 0.033 \times 12$$

$$\text{Moles of 'H'} = 2 \times n_{H_2O} = 0.063$$

$$\text{Mass of 'H'} = 0.063 \times 1 = 0.063

$$\text{Mass of Oxygen (O)} = 1 - (W_c + W_H) = 1 - (0.033 \times 12 + 0.063 \times 1) = 1 - (0.396 + 0.063) = 1 - 0.459 = 0.541

$$\text{Moles of 'O'} = \frac{0.541}{16} = 0.033$$


Empirical formula =  $CH_2O$

Empirical formula mass = 30.

Quinone (a benzene ring with two double bonds and two carbonyl groups) is reduced to quinhydrone (a benzene ring with two hydroxyl groups).](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE Mains 2025 Shift 1 Question Paper Apr 02 With Solutions/345e7c880e3d504980bcde846a8d0428_img.jpg)

Half-cell reaction of quinone (Q) to quinhydrone (QH2). Quinone (a benzene ring with two double bonds and two carbonyl groups) is reduced to quinhydrone (a benzene ring with two hydroxyl groups).

$$[ 	ext{Given : } E_{Ag^+/Ag}^0 = +0.8V 	ext{ and }  rac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.06V 	ext{ }]$$

The  $pK_b$  value of the ammonium halide salt ( $NH_4X$ ) used here is 


$$\text{Ans. } (6)$$

![](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE Mains 2025 Shift 1 Question Paper Apr 02 With Solutions/1d4e90ead2f94b402cdca12cebf8280e_img.jpg)
explanation:
$$QH_2 + 2Ag^+ \rightarrow 2Ag + Q + 2H^+$$

$$E = E^0 - \frac{0.06}{2} \log [H^+]^2$$

$$E = E^0 - 0.06 \times \log [H^+]$$

$$pH = -\log (H^+) = \frac{E - E^0}{0.06} = \frac{0.4 - 0.1}{0.06}$$

$$= \frac{0.3}{0.06} = 5$$

$$pH + NH_4X = 7 - \frac{1}{2} pK_b - \frac{1}{2} \log C$$

$$5 = 7 - \frac{1}{2} \times pK_b - \frac{1}{2} \log (10^{-2})$$

$$pK_b = 6.$$

![Graph showing the relationship between cell potential and pH.](https://tripple-score.sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com/TripleScore/JEE Mains 2025 Shift 1 Question Paper Apr 02 With Solutions/1d4e90ead2f94b402cdca12cebf8280e_img.jpg)

$$K = \frac{1}{2 	imes 76.92}$$

$$[A]_{10} = -Kt + A_0 = -\frac{1}{2 	imes 76.92} 	imes 10 + 2.5 = 2.435$$

$$= 2435 	imes 10^{-3} 	ext{ mol/L}$$
